title: Topological sort Kahn
description: Perform topological sort using Kahn's algorithm (with additional brute-force and DFS approaches for comparison)

examples.explanation: Start with empty path [], visited={}. Kahn's algorithm: compute indegrees [0,1,1]. Queue=[0]. Process 0 → order=[0], decrement 1 & 2 → queue=[1,2]. Process 1 → order=[0,1], decrement 2. Process 2 → order=[0,1,2]. All nodes processed.
n=3, edges=[[0,1],[0,2]]. Indegrees [0,1,1]. Queue=[0]. Process 0 → order=[0], decrement 1 & 2 → queue=[1,2]. Can process 1 then 2 or 2 then 1 → valid order e.g. [0,1,2].

approaches.name: Approach 1: Brute Force DFS with Backtracking
approaches.summary: Approach 1 (Educational): Brute-force DFS with backtracking to generate **all** possible topological orderings (for understanding constraints)

ladders.title: Full Problem — Brute Force DFS with Backtracking for Topological Sort
ladders.desc: Generate all possible topological orderings of a directed graph using DFS with backtracking, detecting cycles.
ladders.explanation: This approach explores all permutations of nodes to find valid topological orderings. The intuition is that topological sort requires linear ordering where all edges point forward—we can brute-force check every possible ordering. We use DFS to recursively build orderings, backtracking when we encounter a cycle (detected via visited set) or when the current partial ordering violates edge constraints. At each step, we try adding an unvisited node to the current path, checking if all its incoming edges come from nodes already in the path. If valid, we recurse; otherwise, we backtrack. The algorithm terminates when we've explored all nodes or found all orderings. Time complexity is O(n!) because we explore all permutations of n nodes. Space complexity is O(n) for recursion stack and visited tracking. This connects to L1 by implementing the complete recursive exploration. Common mistakes include forgetting to backtrack visited nodes, incorrect cycle detection, or not checking edge constraints properly. Real code pattern: def dfs(path, visited): if len(path)==n: record ordering; for node in nodes: if node not visited and all(incoming nodes in path): visited.add(node); path.append(node); dfs(path, visited); path.pop(); visited.remove(node).

examples.trace: Start with empty path [], visited={}. Try node 0: incoming edges none, valid. Path=[0], visited={0}. Recurse: Try node 1: incoming edge from 0 in path, valid. Path=[0,1], visited={0,1}. Recurse: Try node 2: incoming edge from 1 in path, valid. Path=[0,1,2], visited={0,1,2}. Record [0,1,2]. Backtrack: remove 2, visited={0,1}. No more nodes, backtrack remove 1, visited={0}. Try node 2: incoming edge from 1 not in path, invalid. Backtrack remove 0. Try node 1: incoming edge from 0 not in path, invalid. Try node 2: invalid. End.

Start path=[], visited={}. Try 0: valid, path=[0], visited={0}. Recurse: Try 1: incoming from 0 in path, valid, path=[0,1], visited={0,1}. Recurse: Try 2: incoming from 0 in path, valid, path=[0,1,2], record [0,1,2]. Backtrack remove 2, visited={0,1}. No more nodes, backtrack remove 1. Try 2: valid, path=[0,2], visited={0,2}. Recurse: Try 1: valid, path=[0,2,1], record [0,2,1]. Backtrack etc. End.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Recursive DFS with Backtracking for Partial Orderings
ladders.desc: Implement DFS to explore all valid partial orderings, backtracking on invalid states.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine recursively builds topological orderings by adding one node at a time. The intuition is that we maintain a current path (partial ordering) and visited set, and at each step, we try to extend the path with a node that has all its prerequisites (incoming edges) already in the path. We use backtracking to undo choices when they lead to dead ends (e.g., cycles or invalid orderings). The algorithm: base case—if path length equals number of nodes, record ordering; recursive case—iterate through unvisited nodes, check if all incoming edges are from nodes in path, if yes, add node to path and visited, recurse, then backtrack by removing node. Time complexity for this sub-problem is O(n!) in worst case as it explores permutations. Space is O(n) for recursion. This feeds into L0 by being the core recursive engine. Common mistakes: not removing nodes during backtracking, incorrect prerequisite check. Real code: def dfs(path): if len(path)==n: results.append(path.copy()); for node in range(n): if node not in visited and all(u in path for u in incoming[node]): visited.add(node); path.append(node); dfs(path); path.pop(); visited.remove(node).

examples.trace: Current path=[0], visited={0}. Check node 1: incoming [0] all in path, valid. Add 1: path=[0,1], visited={0,1}. Recurse: Check node 2: incoming [1] in path, valid. Add 2: path=[0,1,2], visited={0,1,2}. Base case: record [0,1,2]. Backtrack remove 2, visited={0,1}. No more nodes, backtrack remove 1, visited={0}. Check node 2: incoming [1] not in path, invalid. End.

Start path=[], visited={}. Try node 0: incoming [], valid. Path=[0], visited={0}. Recurse: Try node 1: incoming [], valid. Path=[0,1], record. Backtrack remove 1, visited={0}. No more nodes, backtrack remove 0. Try node 1: valid, path=[1], visited={1}. Recurse: Try node 0: valid, path=[1,0], record. End.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Checking Node Prerequisites During DFS
ladders.desc: Determine if a node can be added to the current path by verifying all its incoming edges are satisfied.
ladders.explanation: The central insight is that in topological sort, a node can only appear after all nodes with edges pointing to it (prerequisites) are already in the ordering. During DFS, before adding a node to the current path, we check if every node in its incoming edge list is present in the path. This ensures the partial ordering remains valid. The intuition: topological ordering is a linear extension of a partial order; this check enforces that constraint incrementally. Algorithm: given current path list and node's incoming edges array, iterate through incoming edges, return True if all are in path, else False. Time O(k) where k is indegree of node. Space O(1). This feeds into L1 by enabling valid node selection. Common mistakes: not handling empty incoming edges, using wrong data structure for path lookup. Real code: def can_add(node, path, incoming): for prereq in incoming[node]: if prereq not in path: return False; return True.

examples.trace: Check prerequisite 0: 0 is in path [0,1]? Yes. Check prerequisite 1: 1 is in path? Yes. All prerequisites satisfied, return True.
Check 0: 0 in [1,0]? Yes. Check 1: 1 in path? Yes. Return True (order in path doesn't matter).

ladders.title: Building Block — Representing Graph Incoming Edges
ladders.desc: Construct an adjacency list for incoming edges from a list of directed edges.
ladders.explanation: This intermediate concept involves building a data structure that maps each node to a list of nodes that have edges pointing to it (incoming edges). The intuition: for topological sort, we need to know prerequisites of each node efficiently. Given n nodes and a list of edges [u,v] meaning u->v, we create an array 'incoming' of size n, where incoming[v] contains all u such that edge u->v exists. This supports quick prerequisite checks during DFS. Algorithm: initialize incoming as list of empty lists for n nodes; for each edge [u,v], append u to incoming[v]. Time O(e) where e is number of edges. Space O(n+e). This feeds into L2 by providing the incoming edge data. Common mistakes: off-by-one indexing, not initializing lists properly. Real code: incoming = [[] for _ in range(n)]; for u,v in edges: incoming[v].append(u).

examples.trace: Initialize incoming=[[],[],[]]. Edge [0,1]: append 0 to incoming[1] -> incoming=[[],[0],[]]. Edge [1,2]: append 1 to incoming[2] -> incoming=[[],[0],[1]].

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Iterating Through Unvisited Nodes
ladders.desc: Loop through all nodes and select those not yet visited in the current DFS path.
ladders.explanation: This fundamental operation involves scanning all nodes in the graph and identifying which ones are not currently in the visited set during DFS recursion. The intuition: at each step of building a topological ordering, we need to consider candidate nodes that haven't been placed yet. We use a visited set (or boolean array) to track nodes already in the current path. The algorithm: for node from 0 to n-1, if node not in visited, it's a candidate for addition. Time O(n) per recursive call. Space O(1) for iteration. This feeds into L3 by providing the node selection mechanism. Common mistakes: modifying visited set during iteration, not resetting properly. Real code: for node in range(n): if node not in visited: # process node.

examples.trace: Iterate node=0: 0 in visited? Yes, skip. Node=1: in visited? Yes, skip. Node=2: not in visited, candidate.
Node=0: not visited, candidate. Node=1: candidate. Node=2: candidate. Node=3: candidate.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Directed Graphs and Nodes
ladders.desc: Understand directed graphs as collections of nodes with directed edges between them.
ladders.explanation: This is the simplest prerequisite: a directed graph consists of nodes (vertices) and directed edges (arrows) from one node to another. The intuition: nodes represent entities, and edges represent dependencies or precedence relations (e.g., task A must be done before task B). For topological sort, we use a directed acyclic graph (DAG). Key concepts: nodes are typically numbered 0 to n-1; an edge u->v means u precedes v. This foundation is needed for all higher levels. No algorithm here—just conceptual understanding. This feeds into L4 by defining what nodes are. Common mistakes: confusing directed with undirected edges. Real code: n = number of nodes; edges = list of [u,v] pairs.

examples.trace: Nodes: 0,1,2. Edge 0->1: from node 0 to node 1. Edge 1->2: from node 1 to node 2. Visualize as 0 → 1 → 2.
Nodes: 0 and 1. Edge from 0 to 1. Direction matters: 0 must come before 1 in ordering.

approaches.name: Approach 2: Kahn's Algorithm with Indegree Tracking
approaches.summary: This method iteratively removes nodes with zero indegree from the graph, adding them to the topological order and updating indegrees of adjacent nodes. It efficiently detects cycles and produces a valid ordering if one exists, ideal for directed acyclic graphs with adjacency lists.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Topological Sort Using Kahn's Algorithm
ladders.desc: Complete implementation of Kahn's algorithm to perform topological sort on a directed graph.
ladders.explanation: Kahn's algorithm provides an efficient method for topological sorting by iteratively removing nodes with zero indegree. The intuition is that in a Directed Acyclic Graph (DAG), there must be at least one node with no incoming edges (indegree 0) that can be placed first in the ordering. By repeatedly removing such nodes and updating the indegrees of their neighbors, we build a valid topological order. This approach naturally detects cycles: if the algorithm terminates before processing all nodes, a cycle exists. The algorithm uses a queue to manage nodes with zero indegree, ensuring O(V+E) time complexity by visiting each node and edge once. Space complexity is O(V) for storing indegrees and the queue. Connection to higher levels: This integrates all sub-components—indegree calculation, queue management, neighbor processing, and cycle detection—into a complete solution. Common mistakes include forgetting to update indegrees after removing nodes, incorrect initialization of indegrees, or mishandling disconnected graphs. Real code pattern: Initialize indegree array, build adjacency list, use queue for zero-indegree nodes, process neighbors, and check if all nodes were processed.

examples.trace: Step 1: Build adjacency list: {0:[1,2], 1:[3], 2:[3], 3:[]}. Indegrees: [0,1,1,2]. Step 2: Initialize queue with node 0 (indegree 0). Step 3: Process node 0: add to order [0], decrement indegrees of neighbors 1 and 2 to [0,0,0,2]. Add nodes 1 and 2 to queue. Step 4: Process node 1: add to order [0,1], decrement indegree of neighbor 3 to [0,0,0,1]. Step 5: Process node 2: add to order [0,1,2], decrement indegree of neighbor 3 to [0,0,0,0]. Add node 3 to queue. Step 6: Process node 3: add to order [0,1,2,3]. Step 7: All nodes processed, return order.

Step 1: Build adjacency list: {0:[1], 1:[0], 2:[]}. Indegrees: [1,1,0]. Step 2: Initialize queue with node 2 (indegree 0). Step 3: Process node 2: add to order [2], no neighbors. Step 4: Queue empty, but nodes 0 and 1 remain unprocessed (indegrees [1,1,0]). Algorithm terminates early, indicating a cycle between nodes 0 and 1. Return empty list.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Processing Nodes with Zero Indegree
ladders.desc: Implement the core loop that removes zero-indegree nodes and updates their neighbors.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine is the heart of Kahn's algorithm, where we repeatedly extract nodes with zero indegree from a queue, add them to the topological order, and decrement the indegrees of their neighbors. The intuition is that removing a node simulates completing a task with no dependencies, allowing dependent tasks to become eligible. The algorithm maintains a queue of nodes ready for processing (indegree 0). For each node dequeued, we append it to the result list and iterate through its outgoing edges. For each neighbor, we reduce its indegree by 1; if the indegree becomes 0, we enqueue the neighbor. This ensures that nodes are processed only when all their dependencies are satisfied. Time complexity is O(V+E) as each node and edge is processed once. Space complexity is O(V) for the queue and result list. Connection to L0: This sub-routine is called within the main algorithm after indegree initialization. Common mistakes include not checking for indegree becoming zero before enqueueing, or modifying the graph structure during iteration. Real code pattern: While queue not empty: node = queue.pop(); result.append(node); for neighbor in graph[node]: indegree[neighbor] -= 1; if indegree[neighbor] == 0: queue.append(neighbor).

examples.trace: Step 1: Dequeue 0, result=[0]. Process neighbors 1 and 2: indegree[1]=0, enqueue 1; indegree[2]=0, enqueue 2. Step 2: Dequeue 1, result=[0,1]. Process neighbor 3: indegree[3]=1. Step 3: Dequeue 2, result=[0,1,2]. Process neighbor 3: indegree[3]=0, enqueue 3. Step 4: Dequeue 3, result=[0,1,2,3]. No neighbors. Queue empty, done.
Step 1: Queue is empty initially (no nodes with indegree 0). While loop never executes, result remains empty. This indicates a cycle where no node can start the processing.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Indegree Calculation and Initialization
ladders.desc: Compute the indegree for each node in a directed graph and identify starting nodes.
ladders.explanation: The core logic of Kahn's algorithm relies on accurately calculating the indegree (number of incoming edges) for each node and initializing a queue with nodes having indegree 0. The intuition is that indegree represents dependencies: a node with indegree 0 has no prerequisites and can be processed first. This step transforms the graph into a dependency map, enabling the iterative removal process. The algorithm iterates through all edges in the graph, incrementing the indegree of the destination node for each edge. After building the indegree array, we scan it to enqueue all nodes with indegree 0. Time complexity is O(V+E) to iterate through nodes and edges. Space complexity is O(V) for the indegree array. Connection to L1: This provides the initial queue and indegree state required for the processing sub-routine. Common mistakes include off-by-one errors in array indices, forgetting to initialize indegrees to 0, or misinterpreting edge direction. Real code pattern: indegree = [0]*V; for each edge (u,v): indegree[v] += 1; queue = [i for i in range(V) if indegree[i] == 0].

examples.trace: Step 1: Initialize indegree=[0,0,0,0]. Step 2: Process edge [1,0]: indegree[1]++ → [0,1,0,0]. Step 3: Process edge [2,0]: indegree[2]++ → [0,1,1,0]. Step 4: Process edge [3,1]: indegree[3]++ → [0,1,1,1]. Step 5: Process edge [3,2]: indegree[3]++ → [0,1,1,2]. Step 6: Scan indegree: node 0 has indegree 0, so queue=[0].

Step 1: Initialize indegree=[0,0,0]. Step 2: Process edge [0,1]: indegree[1]++ → [0,1,0]. Step 3: Process edge [1,2]: indegree[2]++ → [0,1,1]. Step 4: Process edge [2,0]: indegree[0]++ → [1,1,1]. Step 5: Scan indegree: no node has indegree 0, so queue=[].

ladders.title: Building Block — Graph Representation with Adjacency List
ladders.desc: Construct an adjacency list representation of a directed graph from edge lists.
ladders.explanation: This building block involves creating an adjacency list, a fundamental data structure for graph algorithms like Kahn's. An adjacency list represents a graph as an array of lists, where each index corresponds to a node, and its list contains all nodes reachable via outgoing edges. The intuition is that it provides efficient access to a node's neighbors, crucial for updating indegrees during topological sort. For a directed graph, we add edges from source to destination. The algorithm initializes an empty list for each node, then iterates through the edge list, appending the destination to the source's list. Time complexity is O(V+E) to initialize lists and process edges. Space complexity is O(V+E) to store the graph. Connection to L2: The adjacency list is used to compute indegrees and later to access neighbors during processing. Common mistakes include using undirected representation, incorrect indexing, or not handling duplicate edges. Real code pattern: graph = [[] for _ in range(V)]; for u,v in edges: graph[u].append(v).

examples.trace: Step 1: Initialize graph=[[],[],[],[]]. Step 2: Add edge [0,1]: graph[0].append(1) → [[1],[],[],[]]. Step 3: Add edge [0,2]: graph[0].append(2) → [[1,2],[],[],[]]. Step 4: Add edge [1,3]: graph[1].append(3) → [[1,2],[3],[],[]]. Step 5: Add edge [2,3]: graph[2].append(3) → [[1,2],[3],[3],[]. Final graph: [[1,2],[3],[3],[]].

Step 1: Initialize graph=[[],[],[]]. Step 2: Add edge [0,1]: graph[0].append(1) → [[1],[],[]]. Step 3: Add edge [1,2]: graph[1].append(2) → [[1],[2],[]]. Step 4: Add edge [2,0]: graph[2].append(0) → [[1],[2],[0]].

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Queue Management for Node Processing
ladders.desc: Implement a queue to manage nodes in First-In-First-Out (FIFO) order for graph traversal.
ladders.explanation: This basic operation involves using a queue data structure to manage nodes with zero indegree in Kahn's algorithm. The intuition is that a queue ensures nodes are processed in the order they become ready, maintaining a systematic traversal. In Kahn's algorithm, we enqueue nodes when their indegree drops to 0 and dequeue them for processing. This FIFO behavior helps produce a valid topological order, though any order of zero-indegree nodes is acceptable. The algorithm typically uses a list or collections.deque for efficiency. Key operations are enqueue (append) and dequeue (pop from front). Time complexity is O(1) per operation. Space complexity is O(V) in worst case. Connection to L3: The queue is populated after building the adjacency list and indegree calculation. Common mistakes include using a stack (LIFO) which can work but isn't standard, or inefficient dequeue operations. Real code pattern: from collections import deque; queue = deque(); queue.append(node); node = queue.popleft().

examples.input: Initial queue=[0], operations: dequeue, enqueue 1 and 2, dequeue, dequeue, enqueue 3, dequeue
examples.trace: Step 1: Start queue=[0]. Step 2: Dequeue → process node 0, queue=[]. Step 3: Enqueue 1 and 2 → queue=[1,2]. Step 4: Dequeue → process node 1, queue=[2]. Step 5: Dequeue → process node 2, queue=[]. Step 6: Enqueue 3 → queue=[3]. Step 7: Dequeue → process node 3, queue=[].
Step 1: Start queue=[2,0]. Step 2: Dequeue → process node 2, queue=[0]. Step 3: Dequeue → process node 0, queue=[].

testCases.input: Initial queue=[1], operations: dequeue, enqueue 2, dequeue
Initial queue=[3], operations: dequeue, enqueue 4, enqueue 5, dequeue, dequeue
Initial queue=[], operations: enqueue 0, enqueue 1, dequeue, dequeue
Initial queue=[0,1,2], operations: dequeue, dequeue, dequeue
Initial queue=[5], operations: dequeue, enqueue 6, dequeue
Initial queue=[1,2,3], operations: dequeue, enqueue 4, dequeue, dequeue, dequeue

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Understanding Directed Graphs and Edges
ladders.desc: Learn the basics of directed graphs, nodes, and edges as prerequisites for topological sort.
ladders.explanation: This foundation concept introduces directed graphs, which are essential for understanding topological sort. A directed graph consists of nodes (vertices) and edges, where each edge has a direction from a source node to a destination node. The intuition is that direction represents dependencies or precedence: if there's an edge from A to B, then A must come before B in a topological order. Nodes represent entities (e.g., tasks), and edges represent relationships (e.g., task A must be completed before task B). Key terms include indegree (number of incoming edges) and outdegree (number of outgoing edges). For example, in a graph with nodes 0,1 and edge 0→1, node 0 has outdegree 1 and indegree 0, while node 1 has indegree 1 and outdegree 0. This concept is visual and can be represented using diagrams or adjacency lists. Time and space analysis isn't applicable here as it's conceptual. Connection to L4: This understanding is needed to build adjacency lists and compute indegrees. Common mistakes include confusing directed with undirected graphs or misinterpreting edge direction. Real code pattern: Represent edges as pairs (u,v) meaning u→v.

examples.trace: Step 1: Identify nodes: 0,1,2. Step 2: Edge 0→1: draw arrow from node 0 to node 1. Step 3: Edge 1→2: draw arrow from node 1 to node 2. Step 4: Analyze: Node 0 has outdegree 1, indegree 0; node 1 has outdegree 1, indegree 1; node 2 has outdegree 0, indegree 1.

examples.input: Nodes: A,B,C; Edges: A→B, B→C, C→A
examples.trace: Step 1: Identify nodes: A,B,C. Step 2: Edge A→B: arrow A to B. Step 3: Edge B→C: arrow B to C. Step 4: Edge C→A: arrow C to A. Step 5: Analyze: This forms a cycle where A depends on C, C depends on B, B depends on A, so no node can start first.

testCases.expected: n=3, edges=[[0,1],[0,2]] → Node 0 points to 1 and 2

testCases.input: Nodes: A,B,C,D; Edges: A→B, B→C, C→D

testCases.expected: n=3, edges=[[0,1],[2,1]] → Nodes 0 and 2 point to 1

approaches.name: Approach 3: DFS with Postorder Traversal
approaches.summary: This approach uses depth-first search to traverse the graph, marking nodes as visited and adding them to the order in postorder (after processing all dependencies). It recursively explores nodes and can detect cycles using three-state marking, offering an alternative recursive perspective to Kahn's algorithm.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Topological Sort Using DFS with Postorder Traversal
ladders.desc: Complete implementation of topological sort using DFS with postorder traversal and cycle detection.
ladders.explanation: This approach performs topological sorting by recursively exploring the graph using depth-first search (DFS). The key insight is that in a directed acyclic graph (DAG), if we process nodes in postorder (after all their dependencies have been visited), we get a valid topological order when reversed. We maintain three states for each node: UNVISITED (0), VISITING (1), and VISITED (2). The VISITING state is crucial for cycle detection—if we encounter a node that's currently being visited (state 1), we've found a cycle. The algorithm starts from each unvisited node, recursively explores its neighbors, marks the node as VISITED after all dependencies are processed, and adds it to a result list. Finally, we reverse the result list to get the topological order. Time complexity is O(V + E) where V is vertices and E is edges, as we visit each node and edge once. Space complexity is O(V) for the recursion stack and state array. This approach is particularly elegant because it combines traversal, ordering, and cycle detection in one recursive framework. Common mistakes include forgetting to reverse the result list, not handling disconnected graphs properly by iterating through all nodes, and incorrect cycle detection logic. The connection to the parent problem is that this is the complete solution to topological sort using DFS postorder traversal.

examples.trace: Step 1: Initialize state array [0,0,0,0] and result list []. Step 2: Start DFS from node 0. Recurse to neighbors 1 and 2. Process 1 → recurse to 3 → add 3, add 1. Process 2 → add 2 (3 already visited). Add 0 last. Postorder result [3,1,2,0]. Reverse → [0,1,2,3].

Step 1: Initialize state array [0,0,0] and result list [].
Step 2: Start DFS from node 0 (state 0). Mark as VISITING (1).
Step 3: Explore neighbor 1 (state 0). Mark node 1 as VISITING (1).
Step 4: Explore neighbor 2 (state 0). Mark node 2 as VISITING (1).
Step 5: Explore neighbor 0 (state 1). Since node 0 is VISITING, we detect a cycle. Return False/empty list.
Step 6: Algorithm terminates with empty result indicating cycle.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — DFS Recursive Function with Three-State Marking
ladders.desc: The recursive DFS function that processes nodes and detects cycles using three states.
ladders.explanation: This is the core recursive function that implements the DFS traversal with cycle detection. Each node has three states: UNVISITED (0), VISITING (1), and VISITED (2). When we start exploring a node, we mark it as VISITING. Then we recursively visit all its neighbors. If we encounter a neighbor that's currently VISITING, we've found a cycle and return False. After all neighbors are processed, we mark the node as VISITED and add it to the result list. The VISITING state is crucial because it helps distinguish between nodes that are completely processed (VISITED) and nodes that are in the current recursion path (VISITING). This prevents infinite recursion in cyclic graphs. Time complexity for this function is O(V + E) in total across all calls, but for a single call starting from a node, it's O(1 + outdegree). Space complexity is O(V) for the recursion depth. Connection to L0: This function is called repeatedly in the main algorithm to process each node. Common mistakes include not checking for VISITING state properly, forgetting to mark nodes as VISITED after processing, and not handling the return value for cycle detection correctly.

examples.input: Start from node 0 in graph: 0→1, 1→2, state array initially all 0
examples.trace:
Step 1: dfs(0): state[0]=0 → mark VISITING (1).
Step 2: Explore neighbor 1: dfs(1): state[1]=0 → mark VISITING (1).
Step 3: Explore neighbor 2: dfs(2): state[2]=0 → mark VISITING (1).
Step 4: Node 2 has no neighbors → mark VISITED (2), add to result: [2].
Step 5: Return to dfs(1): mark VISITED (2), add to result: [2,1].
Step 6: Return to dfs(0): mark VISITED (2), add to result: [2,1,0].
Step 7: Function completes.

examples.input: Start from node 0 in graph: 0→1, 1→0, state array initially all 0
examples.trace:
Step 1: dfs(0): state[0]=0 → mark VISITING (1).
Step 2: Explore neighbor 1: dfs(1): state[1]=0 → mark VISITING (1).
Step 3: Explore neighbor 0: state[0]=1 (VISITING) → cycle detected! Return False.
Step 4: Propagation returns False up the recursion stack.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Postorder Processing for Topological Ordering
ladders.desc: The insight that processing nodes after their dependencies yields topological order when reversed.
ladders.explanation: The fundamental insight behind this approach is that in a directed acyclic graph (DAG), if we process nodes in postorder (after all their outgoing neighbors/dependencies have been visited), then reversing that order gives a valid topological sort. Why does this work? In topological sort, we need each node to come before all nodes that depend on it. When we do DFS, we fully explore all descendants before finishing the current node. By recording nodes in the order they finish (postorder), we ensure that dependencies are recorded before the nodes that depend on them. Reversing this list then puts dependencies first. For example, if A depends on B, DFS will finish B before A, so postorder gives [A,B], and reversing gives [B,A] which is correct. This is different from preorder where we record nodes when we first visit them. The key is that postorder naturally captures dependency relationships through recursion. Time complexity for this logic is O(V) for reversing the list. Space is O(V) for the result list. Connection to L1: This logic is implemented in the DFS function by adding nodes to result after recursive calls. Common mistakes include forgetting to reverse the result, confusing preorder with postorder, and not understanding why reversal is necessary.

examples.trace:
Step 1: DFS from 0: visit 1, then 2.
Step 2: Node 2 has no neighbors → add to postorder: [2].
Step 3: Return to node 1 → add to postorder: [2,1].
Step 4: Return to node 0 → add to postorder: [2,1,0].
Step 5: Reverse postorder: [0,1,2] which is topological order (0 before 1 before 2).

Step 1: DFS from 0: visit 1 first.
Step 2: Node 1 has no neighbors → add: [1].
Step 3: Back to 0, visit 2 → add: [1,2].
Step 4: Back to 0 → add: [1,2,0].
Step 5: Reverse: [0,2,1] (valid topological order).
Alternative if visiting 2 first: postorder [2,1,0] → reversed [0,1,2].

ladders.title: Building Block — Three-State Marking for Cycle Detection in DFS
ladders.desc: Using three states (UNVISITED, VISITING, VISITED) to detect cycles during graph traversal.
ladders.explanation: This technique enhances standard DFS by adding a VISITING state to detect cycles in directed graphs. Each node is marked with one of three states: UNVISITED (0) means not yet processed, VISITING (1) means currently in the recursion stack (being explored), and VISITED (2) means completely processed. When we start DFS on a node, we mark it VISITING. As we recursively visit neighbors, if we encounter a node that's VISITING, it means we've returned to a node in the current recursion path—indicating a cycle. If we encounter a VISITED node, we simply skip it. After processing all neighbors, we mark the node VISITED. This is more efficient than other cycle detection methods because it integrates seamlessly with DFS. Time complexity is O(1) per state check. Space is O(V) for the state array. Connection to L2: This state management enables the DFS to work correctly for topological sorting by preventing infinite loops and identifying invalid graphs. Common mistakes include using only two states (which can't detect all cycles), not resetting states properly, and confusing when to mark VISITING vs VISITED.

examples.output: Detect cycle when reaching node 0 again
examples.trace:
Step 1: Node 0: UNVISITED→VISITING.
Step 2: Node 1: UNVISITED→VISITING.
Step 3: Node 2: UNVISITED→VISITING.
Step 4: Node 0: already VISITING → cycle detected!
Step 5: Return False.

examples.output: No cycle, all nodes become VISITED
examples.trace:
Step 1: Node 0: UNVISITED→VISITING.
Step 2: Node 1: UNVISITED→VISITING.
Step 3: Node 2: UNVISITED→VISITING→VISITED.
Step 4: Back to node 1: VISITING→VISITED.
Step 5: Back to node 0: VISITING→VISITED.
Step 6: All nodes VISITED, no cycle.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Graph Representation as Adjacency List
ladders.desc: Representing a directed graph using adjacency lists for efficient DFS traversal.
ladders.explanation: An adjacency list is a way to represent a graph where each node has a list of its outgoing neighbors. For a directed graph with V vertices and E edges, we typically use an array or dictionary of lists. For example, graph[0] = [1,2] means node 0 has edges to nodes 1 and 2. This representation is space-efficient (O(V+E)) and allows quick access to all neighbors of a node, which is essential for DFS. To build it from an edge list, we iterate through edges [u,v] and append v to graph[u]. For topological sort, we only need outgoing edges. Time to build is O(E). Space is O(V+E). Connection to L3: The DFS algorithm uses this adjacency list to traverse neighbors. Common mistakes include creating undirected graphs by mistake, not initializing lists for all nodes, and using inefficient representations like adjacency matrices for sparse graphs.

examples.trace:
Step 1: Initialize graph = [[], [], []].
Step 2: Process edge [0,1]: append 1 to graph[0] → [[1], [], []].
Step 3: Process edge [0,2]: append 2 to graph[0] → [[1,2], [], []].
Step 4: Process edge [1,2]: append 2 to graph[1] → [[1,2], [2], []].
Step 5: Final graph: [[1,2], [2], []].

Step 1: Initialize graph = [[], [], [], []].
Step 2: Process [1,0]: append 0 to graph[1] → [[], [0], [], []].
Step 3: Process [2,0]: append 0 to graph[2] → [[], [0], [0], []].
Step 4: Process [3,1]: append 1 to graph[3] → [[], [0], [0], [1]].
Step 5: Process [3,2]: append 2 to graph[3] → [[], [0], [0], [1,2]].
Step 6: Final graph: [[], [0], [0], [1,2]].

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Depth-First Search (DFS) on Graphs
ladders.desc: Basic DFS traversal algorithm for exploring graph nodes recursively.
ladders.explanation: Depth-First Search (DFS) is a fundamental graph traversal algorithm that explores as far as possible along each branch before backtracking. It starts at a node, visits one neighbor, then recursively visits that neighbor's neighbors, continuing deep before returning. For graphs, we need to track visited nodes to avoid infinite loops. The basic recursive DFS pseudocode is: function dfs(node): if node is visited: return; mark node as visited; for each neighbor in graph[node]: dfs(neighbor). This explores all reachable nodes from the starting point. For disconnected graphs, we may need to call DFS from multiple starting nodes. DFS is like exploring a maze by always taking the first path you see until you hit a dead end, then backtracking. Time complexity is O(V+E) where V is vertices and E is edges, since each node and edge is processed once. Space complexity is O(V) for the recursion stack and visited set. Connection to L4: DFS uses the adjacency list to find neighbors. Common mistakes include not marking nodes as visited before recursion (causing infinite loops), using DFS on cyclic graphs without cycle detection, and not handling disconnected graphs.

examples.trace:
Step 1: Start dfs(0): mark visited, visit neighbor 1.
Step 2: dfs(1): mark visited, visit neighbor 2.
Step 3: dfs(2): mark visited, no neighbors.
Step 4: Return to dfs(1): done.
Step 5: Return to dfs(0): done.
Step 6: Visited nodes: 0,1,2.

Step 1: dfs(0): mark visited.
Step 2: Visit neighbor 1 first: dfs(1): mark visited, no neighbors.
Step 3: Back to 0, visit neighbor 2: dfs(2): mark visited, no neighbors.
Step 4: Order: 0,1,2.
Alternative if visiting 2 first: order 0,2,1.

testCases.expected: n=3, edges=[[0,1],[1,2]] → Graph with nodes 0,1,2 and edges 0->1, 1->2
n=2, edges=[[0,1]] → Graph with nodes 0,1 and edge 0->1
n=3, edges=[[0,1],[2,1]] → Nodes 0 and 2 point to 1
